primary_diagnosis.name: Large cervical furuncle (cutaneous abscess) with surrounding cellulitis and low-grade systemic features

pathophysiology_summary: A furuncle is a deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle, usually Staphylococcus aureus, that extends into the subcutaneous tissue to form a walled-off collection of pus (a cutaneous abscess). At the nape of the neck the thick dermis and frequent friction favour follicular infection. Over 4 days follicular inflammation has matured into a fluctuant abscess with central pointing (a visible pus head), while bacterial spread into the surrounding dermis and subcutis has produced perilesional cellulitis (erythema extending beyond the induration) and regional lymphadenopathy. The low-grade fever, chills and malaise reflect an early systemic inflammatory response to the localised infection. A mature, fluctuant, pointing abscess will not resolve on antibiotics alone — the definitive treatment is incision and drainage; antibiotics treat the surrounding cellulitis and limit further spread. Untreated, the abscess can rupture uncontrollably, the cellulitis can spread, and systemic toxicity can progress toward bacteraemia/sepsis; a very large or coalescent lesion may evolve into a carbuncle.

differential.diagnosis: furuncle / cutaneous abscess with surrounding cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly enlarging, painful, hot, red nodule with a pointing pus head on the nape of the neck plus spreading erythema is the classic picture of a furuncle maturing to an abscess with perilesional cellulitis.

A large posterior-neck staphylococcal lesion is the typical site for a carbuncle; here it remains a single fluctuant mass without multiple separate draining sinuses, so it borders on but has not yet become a carbuncle.
Can present as an inflamed neck lump, but there is no history of a pre-existing long-standing painless lump or a central punctum, and the rapid primary follicular course fits a furuncle better.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a furuncle/abscess should lead; carbuncle and infected cyst are reasonable to hold while the systemic and spread features are screened.

differential.diagnosis: furuncle / cutaneous abscess with surrounding cellulitis and early systemic features
differential.evidence_basis: Pointing fluctuant abscess + perilesional cellulitis + fever/chills/malaise + tender regional nodes confirms an abscess with spreading soft-tissue infection and an early systemic response.

Neck stiffness + systemic upset mandates an active airway/deep-space screen; excluded here by absence of dysphagia, drooling, trismus, voice change or breathing difficulty and by clearly superficial pointing.

Perilesional erythema is cellulitic, but the central fluctuance and pus point indicate a drainable abscess is also present — this changes management from antibiotics-only to drainage plus antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The critical move is recognising BOTH a drainable abscess (needs I&D) AND surrounding cellulitis with systemic features (needs antibiotics), while actively excluding a deep neck-space infection.

differential.diagnosis: large cervical furuncle (cutaneous abscess) with surrounding cellulitis, systemically stable
differential.evidence_basis: Mature pointing fluctuant abscess requiring incision and drainage, with perilesional cellulitis and low-grade systemic features but stable vital signs — an urgent same-day in-person problem, not an emergency ambulance call.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot drain an abscess; the correct disposition is urgent same-day in-person I&D plus oral antibiotics, with clear sepsis and deep-space/airway safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Systemic features (fever, chills, malaise) accompanying a soft-tissue infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic upset signals the infection is no longer purely local and raises the risk of progression to bacteraemia/sepsis; it moves the disposition from self-care to urgent same-day clinician assessment and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treating as a simple boil risks progression to spreading cellulitis and systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Perilesional cellulitis — erythema spreading beyond the abscess induration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Spreading erythema indicates soft-tissue infection extending beyond the follicle, requiring systemic antibiotics and review; rapid spread suggests worsening infection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to cover the cellulitis with antibiotics allows the infection to extend, potentially to extensive cellulitis or necrotising infection.

red_flags.description: Mature fluctuant, pointing abscess — a drainable pus collection
red_flags.clinical_significance: A walled-off pus collection will not resolve on antibiotics alone and needs incision and drainage; telehealth cannot perform this, so it defines the need for same-day in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prescribing antibiotics alone without arranging drainage leaves the abscess to enlarge, rupture uncontrollably, or recur.

red_flags.description: Posterior-neck lesion with neck stiffness — possible deep neck-space infection / airway involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: A neck infection with stiffness must be screened for deep-space/airway spread; here dysphagia, drooling, trismus, voice change and breathing difficulty are all absent and the lesion is clearly superficial and pointing, so a deep neck-space infection is excluded — but the screen is mandatory.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an evolving deep neck-space infection or airway compromise could be rapidly life-threatening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing 'a boil' as trivial self-care and missing that this one is a large pointing abscess with spreading cellulitis and systemic features that needs drainage and antibiotics.
Prescribing oral antibiotics without arranging drainage — a mature fluctuant abscess will not resolve on antibiotics alone; incision and drainage is the definitive treatment.
Advising the patient to squeeze, pop or needle the lesion themselves — this can force infection deeper, seed bacteraemia and worsen spread, especially in the posterior-neck region.
Anchoring on a superficial boil and not actively screening for dysphagia, drooling, trismus, voice change or breathing difficulty that would signal a deep neck-space infection.
Sending a systemically stable patient (stable vitals, no sepsis or airway signs) to an emergency ambulance rather than urgent same-day in-person drainage — over-uses emergency resources.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact temperature and vital signs (clinician-measured Temp 38.2 C, HR 95, BP 130/80, RR 16, SpO2 99%)
True degree of fluctuance and whether the collection is drainable on palpation
Tender regional (posterior cervical) lymphadenopathy on palpation
Precise extent of induration and cellulitis margins
Depth of the collection and exclusion of deep fascial involvement by examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a mature pointing abscess with surrounding cellulitis and early systemic features; arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) for incision and drainage plus commencement of oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (e.g. flucloxacillin), with explicit safety-netting to ED/000 if systemic sepsis or airway/deep-space features develop. Do NOT advise self-drainage.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the clinical picture is classic); the real risk is disposition error — under-triaging to self-care or antibiotics-only and failing to arrange drainage, or over-triaging a stable patient to 000.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic — boils, furuncles and cutaneous abscess
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Incision and drainage is the primary treatment for a fluctuant cutaneous abscess; oral antibiotics (anti-staphylococcal, e.g. flucloxacillin) are added when there is surrounding cellulitis, systemic features, or the lesion is large or in a high-risk site.
Mild cellulitis in an immunocompetent adult is treated with oral flucloxacillin; review at 48 hours to confirm response and escalate if spreading or systemic deterioration.
